あ.tip: Start with the horizontal stroke, then the vertical, then the curve.

い.tip: Two strokes: left down, then right down.

う.tip: Vertical stroke first, then the curve from top right.

え.tip: One horizontal, then the angled stroke with a hook.

お.tip: Horizontal, then the crossing stroke, then the loop.

か.tip: Horizontal and vertical cross, then the curve on the right.

き.tip: Two horizontals, then vertical through them, then the hook.

く.tip: Single stroke: angle down from left to right.

こ.tip: Two horizontal strokes, one above the other.

さ.tip: Cross shape first, then the curved part on the right.

し.tip: Single curved stroke from top to bottom.

す.tip: Vertical stroke, then the loop at the bottom.

せ.tip: Vertical line with two horizontals crossing it.

そ.tip: One continuous stroke: curve and zigzag.

た.tip: Two horizontals, vertical, then the small cross at bottom.

ち.tip: Curved stroke on the left, then the right part.

つ.tip: Single stroke: small curve opening to the right.

て.tip: One stroke: horizontal, then down and hook.

と.tip: Horizontal stroke, then the curved part below.

な.tip: Cross, then the vertical with a curve and loop.

に.tip: Vertical stroke, then two horizontals on the right.

ぬ.tip: Curve on the left, then the loop crossing it.

ね.tip: Vertical with curve, then the loop at the bottom.

の.tip: Single stroke: circle with a small opening.

は.tip: Vertical line, then two horizontals, then the right curve.

ひ.tip: Two curved strokes forming an arc shape.

ふ.tip: Horizontal, then three strokes: vertical and two curves.

へ.tip: Single stroke: diagonal down to the right.

ほ.tip: Vertical, two horizontals, then the right side curve.

ま.tip: Vertical through two horizontals, then the right curve.

み.tip: Vertical stroke, then the curved part with a loop.

む.tip: Horizontal, vertical, then the loop at the bottom.

め.tip: Curved stroke on the left, then the right curve with loop.

も.tip: Vertical through two horizontals, then the bottom curve.

や.tip: Left stroke first, then the right stroke crossing it.

ゆ.tip: Curved stroke on the left, then the loop on the right.

よ.tip: Vertical stroke, then the curved part at the bottom.

ら.tip: Short horizontal, then the curved stroke below.

り.tip: Two vertical strokes with a small hook at the top.

る.tip: One stroke: loop with a tail at the bottom.

れ.tip: Vertical with a hook, then the curved part on the right.

ろ.tip: Single stroke: curve and zigzag similar to る but simpler.

わ.tip: Vertical with a hook, then the right curve.

を.tip: Horizontal, then the vertical and the curve (used as object particle).

が.tip: Same as か with voiced mark (゛).

ぎ.tip: Same as き with voiced mark (゛).

ぐ.tip: Same as く with voiced mark (゛).

げ.tip: Same as け with voiced mark (゛).

ご.tip: Same as こ with voiced mark (゛).

ざ.tip: Same as さ with voiced mark (゛).

じ.tip: Same as し with voiced mark (゛).

ず.tip: Same as す with voiced mark (゛).

faq.question: Can I study Katakana row by row?
faq.answer: Yes. The chart is grouped for step-by-step study, with pronunciation and stroke guidance.

faq.question: Can I print Katakana writing worksheets?
faq.answer: Yes. You can generate printable practice sheets for daily writing review.

faq.question: Is this Katakana chart free to use?
faq.answer: Yes. It works in your browser without signup.

sections.title: 1. How can I browse katakana in gojūon order and open writing tips here?
items: View all 46 basic katakana in gojūon order grouped by row (ア行 through ワ行). Click or tap a character to load its details in the right panel.
Use the Play button to hear the character pronounced in Japanese. Use Replay to watch the stroke animation again and hear pronunciation.
When a ハ-row character is selected, use Dakuon and Handakuon buttons to switch to voiced variants (バ, パ, etc.).
Generate and print the handwriting practice sheet for offline practice. Choose basic, basic+dakuon, or full (basic+dakuon+handakuon).

sections.title: 2. How does this katakana study view load reference content in the browser?
items: The chart lists all 46 basic katakana in standard order. Pronunciation uses your browser's speech synthesis (ja-JP). No data is sent to external servers.
Each character shows stroke order animation (template fallback) and a writing tip. Study one row at a time (e.g. ア行, then カ行).

sections.title: 3. What is katakana, and how does this chart help beginners read loanwords?
items: Free reference tool for Japanese katakana: characters, romaji, and writing tips in one place. For beginners and quick lookup.

sections.title: 4. Why keep an online katakana chart open while studying menus or names?
items: All-in-one: characters, romaji, and writing tips.
Dakuon and Handakuon variant switching for ハ row.
No account required.
Offline-ready after first load.

sections.title: 5. When should learners pair katakana drills with hiragana and real reading?
items: Learning Japanese: Learn katakana formation and correct pronunciation.
Quick reference: Find a character by row, confirm romaji.
Handwriting practice: Generate and print practice sheets with various character sets.

ア.tip: Diagonal stroke down-right, then the vertical stroke on the right.

イ.tip: Short diagonal, then the longer vertical stroke.

ウ.tip: Short horizontal, then two strokes forming the top and right side.

エ.tip: Horizontal, then vertical through it, then the bottom horizontal.

オ.tip: Horizontal, then the vertical and the curve on the right.

カ.tip: Horizontal and vertical cross, then the angled stroke on the right.

キ.tip: Two horizontals, then the vertical through them with a hook.

ク.tip: Horizontal stroke, then the angled stroke below.

ケ.tip: Vertical, then two horizontals with the right stroke angled.

コ.tip: Top horizontal, then the vertical and bottom horizontal.

サ.tip: Cross shape first, then the two strokes on the right.

シ.tip: Three short strokes: two on top, one below (like 3 dots).

ス.tip: Horizontal with a hook, then the diagonal stroke.

セ.tip: Vertical with a curve, then the horizontal at the bottom.

ソ.tip: Two diagonals: top-right down, then lower-left down.

タ.tip: Horizontal, then vertical, then the two strokes at bottom.

チ.tip: Horizontal, then the curved stroke and the dot.

ツ.tip: Three short strokes: two on top, one longer below.

テ.tip: Horizontal stroke, then the vertical with a hook on the right.

ト.tip: Vertical stroke, then the small horizontal at top right.

ナ.tip: Horizontal, then the diagonal stroke down-left.

ニ.tip: Two horizontal strokes, one above the other.

ヌ.tip: Horizontal with a curve, then the diagonal stroke.

ネ.tip: Horizontal, then the vertical with two cross strokes.

ノ.tip: Single diagonal stroke from top-left down.

ハ.tip: Two diagonals: left down-right, right down-left.

ヒ.tip: Vertical stroke, then the horizontal with a curve.

フ.tip: Single stroke: horizontal then curve down.

ヘ.tip: Single curved stroke (same as hiragana へ).

ホ.tip: Vertical through two horizontals, then the two strokes on the right.

マ.tip: Horizontal, then the diagonal and horizontal below.

ミ.tip: Three horizontal strokes with a vertical through them.

ム.tip: Horizontal stroke, then the diagonal down-left.

メ.tip: Two diagonals crossing: one down-right, one down-left.

モ.tip: Horizontal, then vertical through it, then the bottom horizontal.

ヤ.tip: Vertical stroke, then the two diagonals on the right.

ユ.tip: Horizontal with a hook, then the vertical stroke.

ヨ.tip: Vertical, then top horizontal, then bottom horizontal.

ラ.tip: Horizontal, then the vertical with a hook.

ル.tip: Vertical with a hook, then the small stroke at bottom.

レ.tip: Vertical stroke, then the angled stroke (like ノ).

ロ.tip: Vertical, top horizontal, then bottom horizontal (box shape).

ワ.tip: Vertical with a hook, then the horizontal at bottom.

ヲ.tip: Horizontal, then vertical, then the small stroke on the right.

ン.tip: Horizontal stroke, then the diagonal down-right.

ガ.tip: Same as カ with voiced mark (゛).

tools.language.hiragana-katakana-convert.metaDescription: Convert text to Hiragana and Katakana at the same time. This online kana converter runs in your browser and supports mixed input.
tools.language.hiragana-katakana-convert.intro: Convert input text into Hiragana and Katakana instantly for study notes, chat, and worksheet prep.
tools.language.hiragana-katakana-convert.guideIntroBefore: Need Korean pronunciation mapping too? Check

faq.question: Can I get both Hiragana and Katakana from one input?
faq.answer: Yes. Enter text once and the tool outputs Hiragana and Katakana side by side.

faq.question: Does this kana converter support romaji input?
faq.answer: Yes. Romaji input is converted in real time, and mixed text is also handled.

faq.question: Is this converter private?
faq.answer: Yes. Conversion runs locally in your browser.

sections.title: 1. How can I get Hiragana and Katakana together from mixed input on this page?
items: Enter text in the input area (romaji, Hiragana, Katakana, or mixed).
Review the Hiragana output in the first box and the Katakana output in the second.
Click Copy next to the result you need and paste it into your document or app.

sections.title: 2. How does this converter handle romaji, English, and Korean locally in the browser?
items: The converter processes your input in real time and produces two outputs: one in Hiragana and one in Katakana. Romaji (e.g. konnichiwa, sushi) is converted to kana using standard Japanese romanization.

Existing Hiragana or Katakana in the input can be echoed or normalized. Hiragana (ひらがな) has 46 basic characters and is used for native Japanese words. Katakana (カタカナ) also has 46 basic characters and is typically used for loanwords and foreign names.
Pronunciation uses your browser's built-in conversion. No data is sent to external servers.

sections.title: 3. What is this Hiragana and Katakana converter for, and why kana only (no kanji)?
items: Free online Hiragana Katakana converter that transforms text into Japanese kana from input such as romaji (Latin script), English, or existing kana. This tool outputs kana only and does not use or produce kanji (Chinese characters).

sections.title: 4. Why use one tool that outputs both Hiragana and Katakana side by side?
items: Dual output: Get both Hiragana and Katakana from one input without switching modes.
Free and private: No signup required; conversion runs in your browser.
Instant: Real-time conversion as you type.
Supports Korean: Hangul input is converted to romanization first, then to kana.

sections.title: 5. When do learners need instant Hiragana and Katakana for lyrics, chat, or class?
items: Language learning: Convert words or sentences from romaji to kana for reading and typing practice.
Vocabulary: Generate Hiragana or Katakana versions for teaching or self-study.
Content creation: Produce kana-only text for exercises, worksheets, and flashcards.
Quick lookup: Check the kana spelling of a word when you know only the romaji.

ui.inputPlaceholder: Enter text in any language (romaji, English, Korean, etc.)...

tools.language.hangul-to-hiragana.subtitle: Map Korean Hangul syllables to hiragana by pronunciation
tools.language.hangul-to-hiragana.metaDescription: Convert Hangul to Hiragana by pronunciation with an online browser-based tool. Useful for quick Korean-to-kana study notes.
tools.language.hangul-to-hiragana.intro: Turn Korean Hangul into hiragana that follows approximate pronunciation. Non-Hangul characters pass through unchanged.

faq.question: Is this a direct translation tool?
faq.answer: No. It converts Hangul to Hiragana by approximate pronunciation, not full language translation.

faq.question: What happens to non-Hangul characters?
faq.answer: Latin letters, numbers, spaces, and punctuation pass through unchanged.

faq.question: Can I convert the result back to Hangul?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the Hiragana to Hangul page for inverse pronunciation-style decoding.

sections.title: 1. How can I convert Korean Hangul to Hiragana pronunciation on this page?
items: Type or paste text that includes Korean Hangul in the input area.
Read the Hiragana output, which approximates how each Hangul syllable would be pronounced using Japanese kana.
Use Copy to paste the result into notes, flashcards, or chat.

sections.title: 2. How does Hangul-to-Hiragana mapping run locally in my browser?
items: Each Hangul syllable is split into an initial consonant, a vowel (or diphthong), and an optional final consonant (batchim).
Those pieces are mapped to hiragana that roughly match Korean sounds—not official Korean or Japanese spelling rules.
Letters that are not Hangul syllables (Latin, punctuation, spaces, numbers) are copied through unchanged.
Everything runs locally in your browser; your text is not sent to a server.

sections.title: 3. What is Hangul to Hiragana conversion for, and what are its limits?
items: This tool is for language learners who want a quick, kana-only rendering of spoken Korean. It is not a substitute for standard romanization (Revised Romanization, McCune–Reischauer) or for official Japanese orthography.
Approximations differ by speaker and context; use dictionaries or classroom materials when precision matters.

sections.title: 4. Why use a browser Hangul to Hiragana converter instead of manual kana tables?
items: Real-time conversion as you type.
Private: no signup, no cloud processing.
Clear separation from the general Hiragana–Katakana converter, which uses a different pipeline.

sections.title: 5. When is Hangul-to-Hiragana output useful for Japanese-Korean learners?
items: Japanese learners comparing Korean sounds to familiar hiragana.
Rough subtitles or study notes when only kana is desired.
Quick checks before consulting a teacher or a pronunciation guide.

ui.inputPlaceholder: Type or paste Korean Hangul (other characters are left unchanged)…
ui.body: This page is a supplementary study aid only. It does not provide reliable translation between Korean and Japanese, and it is not suitable for checking official Korean spelling, standard romanization, or formal rules for how Korean is written in Japanese kana. For anything that requires correctness (publication, schoolwork, names on documents, broadcasting, and similar), use dictionaries, institutional or publisher style guides, or qualified human review.

tools.language.hiragana-to-hangul.subtitle: Decode pronunciation-style hiragana back into Hangul
tools.language.hiragana-to-hangul.metaDescription: Convert Hiragana to Hangul with inverse pronunciation mapping. This online browser tool helps recover Korean-style output from kana notes.
tools.language.hiragana-to-hangul.intro: Decode hiragana (and basic katakana) into Hangul using the inverse of our Hangul→Hiragana pronunciation rules.
tools.language.hiragana-to-hangul.guideIntroBefore: This works best with text generated from
tools.language.hiragana-to-hangul.guideIntroBetween: . If your notes are in katakana, use

faq.question: Is this a full Japanese to Korean translator?
faq.answer: No. It is an inverse pronunciation mapper, mainly for text created with the paired Hangul-to-Hiragana workflow.

faq.question: Can I paste Katakana too?
faq.answer: Yes. Basic Katakana is normalized and processed with the same inverse mapping logic.

faq.question: Will every input decode perfectly?
faq.answer: Not always. Accuracy is best when input matches the pronunciation style produced by the paired forward converter.

sections.title: 1. How can I convert Japanese Hiragana to Hangul pronunciation on this page?
items: Type or paste hiragana that matches the output style of our Hangul to Hiragana tool.
Read the Hangul output. Existing Hangul, Latin letters, numbers, and punctuation are copied unchanged.
Use Copy to take the result elsewhere.

sections.title: 2. How does Hiragana-to-Hangul mapping run locally in my browser?
items: A lookup table is built once from every modern Hangul syllable: each syllable's forward mapping (Hangul→hiragana) becomes a key for the reverse direction.
The parser walks the text, and on each hiragana run it takes the longest matching key so multi-character syllables (e.g. batchim) decode correctly.
Basic katakana syllables (ア–ン range mapped to hiragana) are normalized before matching, so you can paste either script.
If several Hangul syllables share the same hiragana key, the table keeps one representative syllable (lowest code point).

sections.title: 3. What is this Hiragana to Hangul converter for, and how accurate is it?
items: This is the approximate inverse of Hangul to Hiragana (Pronunciation). It is not a general Japanese→Korean translator and does not handle kanji, grammar, or vocabulary.
Strings that were not produced by our forward tool, or that use readings we do not map, may decode incorrectly or leave some kana untouched.

sections.title: 4. Why use a browser Hiragana to Hangul tool for study or quick reference?
items: Round-trip friendly for text produced by Hangul to Hiragana under the same rules.
Private, in-browser processing.

sections.title: 5. When do learners use Hiragana-to-Hangul output with notes or pronunciation drills?
items: Checking whether kana notes line up with intended Korean syllables.
Recovering Hangul from kana that you generated with our Hangul→Hiragana converter.

ui.inputPlaceholder: Type or paste hiragana (main katakana syllables are normalized to hiragana first). Hangul and other characters pass through…
ui.body: This page is a supplementary study aid only. It is not a Korean–Japanese translator, and it must not be used as the sole check for correct Hangul spelling or wording. Decoding uses the approximate inverse of this site's Hangul→kana rules, so output often differs from the original Korean, especially after a round trip. Treat results as rough reference and verify with dictionaries, teachers, or other authoritative sources when accuracy matters.

faq.answer: It is for inverse pronunciation mapping, not full sentence translation.

faq.question: Can I use this with mixed Hiragana and Katakana?
faq.answer: Yes. Mixed kana input works because Katakana is normalized during processing.

faq.question: When is this most accurate?

sections.title: 1. How can I convert Japanese Katakana to Hangul pronunciation on this page?
items: Type or paste katakana that matches the output style of our Hangul to Katakana tool.
Read the Hangul output. Existing Hangul, Latin letters, numbers, and punctuation are copied unchanged.
Use Copy to take the result elsewhere.

sections.title: 2. How does Katakana-to-Hangul mapping run locally in my browser?
items: The engine is the same as Hiragana to Hangul: katakana in the ア–ン syllable range is converted to hiragana (Unicode offset), then longest-prefix matching against keys built from every modern Hangul syllable.
Hiragana-only input works too, so you can use either page interchangeably for mixed or single-script notes.
If several Hangul syllables share the same kana key, the table keeps one representative syllable (lowest code point)—same limitation as the hiragana version.

sections.title: 3. What is this Katakana to Hangul converter for, and how accurate is it?
items: This is the approximate inverse of Hangul to Katakana (Pronunciation), using the same rules as Hangul to Hiragana under the hood.
It is not a general Japanese→Korean translator. Round-trip text may not match the original Korean exactly.

sections.title: 4. Why use a browser Katakana to Hangul tool for study or quick reference?
items: Optimized for learners who took notes in katakana from our Hangul→Katakana converter.
Private, in-browser processing.

sections.title: 5. When do learners use Katakana-to-Hangul output with textbooks or flashcards?
items: Turning katakana practice lines back into Hangul for comparison.
Working next to the Hiragana to Hangul page when your materials use katakana.

items: Choose the utility you need from the list: converter, formatter, color, sprite, or QR tool.
Paste or upload your input, then run the action (convert, format, generate, or scan).
Copy the result immediately or download output files when available.
Switch tools quickly when you are debugging different data formats in one workflow.

sections.title: 2. How do developer tools on this site run locally in my browser?
items: All developer tools run in your browser, so input data stays on your device.
Converters and encoders process text and files in real time with client-side APIs.
Formatting, color picking, sprite generation, and QR handling are all processed locally.

sections.title: 3. What developer tools are available and what problems do they solve?
items: Developer Tools is a fast browser toolkit for everyday coding and debugging tasks.
It helps with encoding, formatting, color values, sprite output, and QR workflows.
No signup or installation is required.

sections.title: 4. Why use browser-based encoders, formatters, and generators?
items: Keeps data local with in-browser processing.
Covers common developer utilities in one place.
Works quickly without account setup.
Supports desktop and mobile usage.

sections.title: 5. When are quick developer utilities faster than a full IDE or CLI?
items: Checking encoded payloads while testing APIs.
Cleaning up code snippets before sharing or committing.
Converting number systems during low-level debugging.
Generating QR codes for links, Wi-Fi, and contact cards.
Scanning QR images captured from docs, slides, or screenshots.

items: The converter maps each character to numeric code values for the selected format.
It supports standard and extended ranges in one interface.
Decoding parses tokens and reconstructs text locally in your browser.

items: Free online ASCII converter for decimal, hexadecimal, and binary workflows.

errorMessages.Only one decimal point is allowed: Only one decimal point is allowed

ui.universalGuideNote: Every dedicated pair below uses this same three-step layout with bases filled in for that page.
ui.pairGridTitle: Dedicated converters (binary, octal, decimal, hexadecimal, character)
ui.pairGridDesc: {count} pages — every directed pair of formats below, with fixed input/output and the same parsing rules as the main converter (including optional fractional input on numeric bases).
ui.faqSectionDesc: {count} quick answers with guides and links to the matching converter.

conversionGuide.intro: Three steps: symbols → the right math move for this pair → worked examples you can copy on paper. Numeric bases (binary, octal, decimal, hex) also support one radix point and digits after it; character mode stays a single code unit.
conversionGuide.hexMapIntro: Hex conversions use this letter-to-number map:
conversionGuide.step3Intro: Two practice values in {fromName}, converted to {toName} using the same rules as Step 2.
conversionGuide.step3FracNote: Example 3 uses a fractional part (digits after the radix point); the hub and pair calculators accept a single . on binary, octal, decimal, and hex inputs.
conversionGuide.parseFail: Example {n}: could not parse {raw} for this pair.
conversionGuide.divFracIntro: Fractional part — multiply by {r}, integer of each product = next digit after .
conversionGuide.step2BridgeIntro: First express the left format as a decimal value (one optional . for a fractional part is allowed on numeric bases), then rewrite that value in the right format. The calculator automates both steps.
conversionGuide.step2SkipPartB: You already start in decimal — skip to Part B.
conversionGuide.step2GoalDecimal: Your goal is decimal — Part A already finished the job.
conversionGuide.step2CharOther: Step 2 — Character Code, Then Target Base
conversionGuide.charToDecBody: Look up the numeric code for your character. That integer is the decimal value for the next step.
conversionGuide.placeWeightsBody: Number positions from the right, starting at 0. At each position, multiply that digit by {radix} raised to the position index, then add every term. The total is your decimal number.
conversionGuide.fractionalBody: Digits after the dot: use negative powers of {radix} ({radix}^-1, {radix}^-2, …). Each place is still (digit × weight); add the fractional side to the whole side.
conversionGuide.hexAfReminder: A–F reminder: treat A as 10, B as 11, …, F as 15 when you multiply by powers of 16 (see Step 1 table).
conversionGuide.hexAfMapping: A–F mapping: use 10–15 for letters when applying place weights.
conversionGuide.decToCharBody: Take the decimal value. If it is in range, the tool maps it to the matching character (or a standard control label).
conversionGuide.divBy2Body: Repeated division by 2: divide the decimal number by 2, write each remainder, replace the number with the whole quotient, repeat until the quotient is 0. Read the remainders from last division to first to read the binary digits.
conversionGuide.divBy8Body: Repeated division by 8: same idea as binary, but divide by 8 each time. Remainders are octal digits (0–7), read bottom-up.
conversionGuide.divBy16Body: Repeated division by 16: each remainder is one hex digit; values 10–15 become A–F. Read remainders from last step to first.
conversionGuide.fracDecBody: Fractional decimal values: convert the whole part with repeated division, then multiply the fractional part by the target base over and over; each integer you get is the next digit after the radix point (same idea the calculator shows in Formulas).

8.beginnerSymbols: Each digit is 0–7 (eight possibilities per position).
8.devNote: A leading 0 is often used to mark octal (e.g. 012).

10.beginnerSymbols: Ordinary digits 0–9; each position is a power of ten.

16.beginnerSymbols: Digits 0–9 plus letters A–F for values ten through fifteen.

char.beginnerSymbols: Exactly one symbol; its numeric code is the value you convert.
char.devNote: This tool uses the UTF-16 code unit (0–65535 for BMP).

tools.developer.color-picker.converter.h.subtitle: Fixed HEX to RGB conversion
tools.developer.color-picker.converter.h.intro: Convert HEX input to RGB output with formula lines and copy-ready result.

sections.title: About HEX
items: Hexadecimal color uses six (or eight with alpha) base-16 digits and is common in CSS and design tools.

sections.title: About RGB
items: RGB represents red, green, and blue channels as 0-255 values in the sRGB color space.

sections.title: Summary
items: The converter parses HEX, normalizes through sRGB, and encodes RGB.

sections.title: Relationship context
items: HEX and RGB describe the same color in different notations.

faq.question: How do I convert HEX to RGB on this page?
faq.answer: Enter a HEX value and the RGB result is generated immediately.

faq.question: Does this conversion run locally?
faq.answer: Yes. Processing runs in your browser.

faq.question: Can I switch to other color pairs?
faq.answer: Yes. Use cross-links below to open other dedicated color converters.

errorMessages.Use rgb(r, g, b) or r, g, b (0–255): Use rgb(r, g, b) or r, g, b (0–255)
errorMessages.RGB channels must be between 0 and 255: RGB channels must be between 0 and 255
errorMessages.Use rgba(r, g, b, a) with a from 0 to 1: Use rgba(r, g, b, a) with a from 0 to 1 (or fourth value 0–255 as alpha)
errorMessages.Use hsl(h, s%, l%) with h 0–360 and s,l: Use hsl(h, s%, l%) with h 0–360 and s,l 0–100
errorMessages.Use hsv(h, s%, v%) with h 0–360 and s,v: Use hsv(h, s%, v%) with h 0–360 and s,v 0–100
errorMessages.Use cmyk(c%, m%, y%, k%) with values 0–1: Use cmyk(c%, m%, y%, k%) with values 0–100
errorMessages.CMYK components must be between 0 and 10: CMYK components must be between 0 and 100

ui.howToIntro1: Enter a valid {from} value in the calculator. The tool parses your input, holds the color as sRGB (with alpha when relevant), then formats the result as {to}.
ui.howToIntro2: Accepted shapes include CSS-like functions (e.g. rgb(...), hsl(...)) and compact comma-separated numbers where appropriate. Hex may include or omit the leading #.
ui.procedureStep1: Parse {from}: validate syntax and channel ranges for this format.
ui.procedureStep2: Resolve to sRGB: convert parsed channels to an internal sRGB sample (alpha when relevant).
ui.procedureStep3: Encode as {to}: derive target notation from the sRGB sample.
ui.procedureStep4: Finalize: round and display the {to} string you can copy from the calculator.
ui.procedureFootnoteCmyk: CMYK is a screen-side print approximation; use HEX/RGB as the on-screen reference for critical work.
ui.procedureFootnoteHsl: Whole-number HSL/HSV values may round slightly versus HEX picked in the Color Picker — expected behavior.
ui.examplesIntro: Sample {from} values and matching {to} output using the same rules as the calculator.
ui.crossLinksIntro: Other dedicated pages (fixed input and output types). Open the Color Picker for the full list.
ui.cmykNoteP1: CMYK describes ink on paper; on screen we still use sRGB. This tool uses a simple 0–100% model for preview—not a specific printer ICC profile—so it will not match Photoshop or print shops exactly.
ui.cmykNoteP2: When CMYK is shown as whole percentages, several different RGB colors can round to the same CMYK string. CMYK→RGB is not unique. CMYK copied from the Color Picker is converted to match the same HEX/RGB shown beside that color.
ui.cmykNoteP3: For web and UI work, treat HEX / RGB as the stable reference; use CMYK as an approximate print-oriented hint.

errorMessages.Use rgb(r, g, b) or r, g, b (0–255): Use rgb(r, g, b) or r, g, b (0–255)
errorMessages.RGB channels must be between 0 and 255: RGB channels must be between 0 and 255
errorMessages.Use rgba(r, g, b, a) with a from 0 to 1: Use rgba(r, g, b, a) with a from 0 to 1 (or fourth value 0–255 as alpha)
errorMessages.Use hsl(h, s%, l%) with h 0–360 and s,l: Use hsl(h, s%, l%) with h 0–360 and s,l 0–100
errorMessages.Use hsv(h, s%, v%) with h 0–360 and s,v: Use hsv(h, s%, v%) with h 0–360 and s,v 0–100
errorMessages.Use cmyk(c%, m%, y%, k%) with values 0–1: Use cmyk(c%, m%, y%, k%) with values 0–100

ui.howToIntro1: Enter a valid {from} value in the calculator. The tool parses your input, holds the color as sRGB (with alpha when relevant), then formats the result as {to}.
ui.howToIntro2: Accepted shapes include CSS-like functions (e.g. rgb(...), hsl(...)) and compact comma-separated numbers where appropriate. Hex may include or omit the leading #.
ui.procedureStep1: Parse {from}: validate syntax and channel ranges for this format.
ui.procedureStep2: Resolve to sRGB: convert parsed channels to an internal sRGB sample (alpha when relevant).
ui.procedureStep3: Encode as {to}: derive target notation from the sRGB sample.
ui.procedureStep4: Finalize: round and display the {to} string you can copy from the calculator.
ui.procedureFootnoteCmyk: CMYK is a screen-side print approximation; use HEX/RGB as the on-screen reference for critical work.
ui.procedureFootnoteHsl: Whole-number HSL/HSV values may round slightly versus HEX picked in the Color Picker — expected behavior.
ui.examplesIntro: Sample {from} values and matching {to} output using the same rules as the calculator.
ui.crossLinksIntro: Other dedicated pages (fixed input and output types). Open the Color Picker for the full list.
ui.cmykNoteP1: CMYK describes ink on paper; on screen we still use sRGB. This tool uses a simple 0–100% model for preview—not a specific printer ICC profile—so it will not match Photoshop or print shops exactly.
ui.cmykNoteP2: When CMYK is shown as whole percentages, several different RGB colors can round to the same CMYK string. CMYK→RGB is not unique. CMYK copied from the Color Picker is converted to match the same HEX/RGB shown beside that color.
ui.cmykNoteP3: For web and UI work, treat HEX / RGB as the stable reference; use CMYK as an approximate print-oriented hint.

tools.developer.numbersystem-converter.b.subtitle: Fixed Binary input to Octal output
tools.developer.numbersystem-converter.b.intro: Convert Binary values to Octal with step-by-step explanation and reference tables.

sections.title: About Binary
items: Binary (base 2) uses digits 0 and 1 and maps directly to bit operations.

sections.title: About Octal
items: Octal (base 8) uses digits 0-7 and groups binary digits in chunks of three.

sections.title: Summary
items: Input is parsed as Binary, normalized, then encoded as Octal.

sections.title: Relationship context
items: Base conversion rewrites the same value in a different radix representation.

faq.question: How do I convert Binary to Octal on this page?
faq.answer: Enter a Binary value and the Octal result is generated automatically.

faq.question: Are fractional values supported?
faq.answer: Numeric bases support one radix point. Character mode requires integer code values.

faq.question: Can I switch to other pair pages quickly?
faq.answer: Yes. Related pair links below let you switch quickly.

messages.formulaPlaceholder: Enter a value → place-value expansion + division chain.

ui.otherPairsIntro: Other fixed input/output converters use the same parsing rules as the hub. Open any pair for the same calculator layout and reference tables.
ui.pairLinkLine2: Fixed input/output · same parsing as hub

conversionGuide.intro: Three steps: symbols → the right math move for this pair → worked examples you can copy on paper. Numeric bases (binary, octal, decimal, hex) also support one radix point and digits after it; character mode stays a single code unit.
conversionGuide.hexMapIntro: Hex conversions use this letter-to-number map:
conversionGuide.step3Intro: Two practice values in {fromName}, converted to {toName} using the same rules as Step 2.
conversionGuide.step3FracNote: Example 3 uses a fractional part (digits after the radix point); the hub and pair calculators accept a single . on binary, octal, decimal, and hex inputs.
conversionGuide.parseFail: Example {n}: could not parse {raw} for this pair.
conversionGuide.divFracIntro: Fractional part — multiply by {r}, integer of each product = next digit after .
conversionGuide.step2BridgeIntro: First express the left format as a decimal value (one optional . for a fractional part is allowed on numeric bases), then rewrite that value in the right format. The calculator automates both steps.
conversionGuide.step2SkipPartB: You already start in decimal — skip to Part B.
conversionGuide.step2GoalDecimal: Your goal is decimal — Part A already finished the job.
conversionGuide.step2CharOther: Step 2 — Character Code, Then Target Base
conversionGuide.charToDecBody: Look up the numeric code for your character. That integer is the decimal value for the next step.
conversionGuide.placeWeightsBody: Number positions from the right, starting at 0. At each position, multiply that digit by {radix} raised to the position index, then add every term. The total is your decimal number.
conversionGuide.fractionalBody: Digits after the dot: use negative powers of {radix} ({radix}^-1, {radix}^-2, …). Each place is still (digit × weight); add the fractional side to the whole side.
conversionGuide.hexAfReminder: A–F reminder: treat A as 10, B as 11, …, F as 15 when you multiply by powers of 16 (see Step 1 table).
conversionGuide.hexAfMapping: A–F mapping: use 10–15 for letters when applying place weights.
conversionGuide.decToCharBody: Take the decimal value. If it is in range, the tool maps it to the matching character (or a standard control label).
conversionGuide.divBy2Body: Repeated division by 2: divide the decimal number by 2, write each remainder, replace the number with the whole quotient, repeat until the quotient is 0. Read the remainders from last division to first to read the binary digits.
conversionGuide.divBy8Body: Repeated division by 8: same idea as binary, but divide by 8 each time. Remainders are octal digits (0–7), read bottom-up.
conversionGuide.divBy16Body: Repeated division by 16: each remainder is one hex digit; values 10–15 become A–F. Read remainders from last step to first.
conversionGuide.fracDecBody: Fractional decimal values: convert the whole part with repeated division, then multiply the fractional part by the target base over and over; each integer you get is the next digit after the radix point (same idea the calculator shows in Formulas).

8.beginnerSymbols: Each digit is 0–7 (eight possibilities per position).
8.devNote: A leading 0 is often used to mark octal (e.g. 012).

10.beginnerSymbols: Ordinary digits 0–9; each position is a power of ten.

16.beginnerSymbols: Digits 0–9 plus letters A–F for values ten through fifteen.

char.beginnerSymbols: Exactly one symbol; its numeric code is the value you convert.
char.devNote: This tool uses the UTF-16 code unit (0–65535 for BMP).

errorMessages.Character input cannot include a decimal: Character input cannot include a decimal point
errorMessages.Only one decimal point is allowed: Only one decimal point is allowed
errorMessages.Value is not a finite number: Value is not a finite number
errorMessages.Character output requires a whole number: Character output requires a whole number (0–65535)
errorMessages.Character code must be between 0 and 655: Character code must be between 0 and 65535

conversionGuide.divBy8Body: Repeated division by 8: same idea as binary, but divide by 8 each time. Remainders are octal digits (0–7), read bottom-up.
conversionGuide.divBy16Body: Repeated division by 16: each remainder is one hex digit; values 10–15 become A–F. Read remainders from last step to first.
conversionGuide.fracDecBody: Fractional decimal values: convert the whole part with repeated division, then multiply the fractional part by the target base over and over; each integer you get is the next digit after the radix point (same idea the calculator shows in Formulas).

8.beginnerSymbols: Each digit is 0–7 (eight possibilities per position).
8.devNote: A leading 0 is often used to mark octal (e.g. 012).

10.beginnerSymbols: Ordinary digits 0–9; each position is a power of ten.

16.beginnerSymbols: Digits 0–9 plus letters A–F for values ten through fifteen.

char.beginnerSymbols: Exactly one symbol; its numeric code is the value you convert.
char.devNote: This tool uses the UTF-16 code unit (0–65535 for BMP).

conversionGuide.hexAfReminder: A–F reminder: treat A as 10, B as 11, …, F as 15 when you multiply by powers of 16 (see Step 1 table).
conversionGuide.hexAfMapping: A–F mapping: use 10–15 for letters when applying place weights.
conversionGuide.decToCharBody: Take the decimal value. If it is in range, the tool maps it to the matching character (or a standard control label).
conversionGuide.divBy2Body: Repeated division by 2: divide the decimal number by 2, write each remainder, replace the number with the whole quotient, repeat until the quotient is 0. Read the remainders from last division to first to read the binary digits.
conversionGuide.divBy8Body: Repeated division by 8: same idea as binary, but divide by 8 each time. Remainders are octal digits (0–7), read bottom-up.
conversionGuide.divBy16Body: Repeated division by 16: each remainder is one hex digit; values 10–15 become A–F. Read remainders from last step to first.
conversionGuide.fracDecBody: Fractional decimal values: convert the whole part with repeated division, then multiply the fractional part by the target base over and over; each integer you get is the next digit after the radix point (same idea the calculator shows in Formulas).

8.beginnerSymbols: Each digit is 0–7 (eight possibilities per position).
8.devNote: A leading 0 is often used to mark octal (e.g. 012).

10.beginnerSymbols: Ordinary digits 0–9; each position is a power of ten.

16.beginnerSymbols: Digits 0–9 plus letters A–F for values ten through fifteen.

char.beginnerSymbols: Exactly one symbol; its numeric code is the value you convert.
char.devNote: This tool uses the UTF-16 code unit (0–65535 for BMP).

conversionGuide.step2BridgeIntro: First express the left format as a decimal value (one optional . for a fractional part is allowed on numeric bases), then rewrite that value in the right format. The calculator automates both steps.
conversionGuide.step2SkipPartB: You already start in decimal — skip to Part B.
conversionGuide.step2GoalDecimal: Your goal is decimal — Part A already finished the job.
conversionGuide.step2CharOther: Step 2 — Character Code, Then Target Base
conversionGuide.charToDecBody: Look up the numeric code for your character. That integer is the decimal value for the next step.
conversionGuide.placeWeightsBody: Number positions from the right, starting at 0. At each position, multiply that digit by {radix} raised to the position index, then add every term. The total is your decimal number.
conversionGuide.fractionalBody: Digits after the dot: use negative powers of {radix} ({radix}^-1, {radix}^-2, …). Each place is still (digit × weight); add the fractional side to the whole side.
conversionGuide.hexAfReminder: A–F reminder: treat A as 10, B as 11, …, F as 15 when you multiply by powers of 16 (see Step 1 table).
conversionGuide.hexAfMapping: A–F mapping: use 10–15 for letters when applying place weights.
conversionGuide.decToCharBody: Take the decimal value. If it is in range, the tool maps it to the matching character (or a standard control label).
conversionGuide.divBy2Body: Repeated division by 2: divide the decimal number by 2, write each remainder, replace the number with the whole quotient, repeat until the quotient is 0. Read the remainders from last division to first to read the binary digits.
conversionGuide.divBy8Body: Repeated division by 8: same idea as binary, but divide by 8 each time. Remainders are octal digits (0–7), read bottom-up.
conversionGuide.divBy16Body: Repeated division by 16: each remainder is one hex digit; values 10–15 become A–F. Read remainders from last step to first.
conversionGuide.fracDecBody: Fractional decimal values: convert the whole part with repeated division, then multiply the fractional part by the target base over and over; each integer you get is the next digit after the radix point (same idea the calculator shows in Formulas).

8.beginnerSymbols: Each digit is 0–7 (eight possibilities per position).
8.devNote: A leading 0 is often used to mark octal (e.g. 012).

10.beginnerSymbols: Ordinary digits 0–9; each position is a power of ten.

16.beginnerSymbols: Digits 0–9 plus letters A–F for values ten through fifteen.

char.beginnerSymbols: Exactly one symbol; its numeric code is the value you convert.
char.devNote: This tool uses the UTF-16 code unit (0–65535 for BMP).
